description: Empty-vs-absent field distinction. The presence of an empty array (tags: []) produces a different canonical form, and therefore a different SHA-256, than the complete absence of the field. Producers MUST be deliberate about emitting versus omitting empty fields.

vectors.name: Body with empty tags array
vectors.description: Body containing tags: [] (empty array, but field present). This is normatively distinct from the absent-tags vector below.

vectors.name: Body without tags field
vectors.description: Same body but with tags omitted entirely. The canonical form is shorter; the SHA-256 differs from the empty-tags case. Note: this vector's hash is identical to can-001 vector 1's hash (5f8d88d6...) because the underlying inputs are equivalent.
vectors.notes: Cross-checks with can-001 vector 1 — the input is bit-identical, so the hash MUST match. Implementations that fail this consistency check have a determinism bug somewhere in their canonicalization or hashing pipeline.

implementer_notes: Producers SHOULD pick one convention and stick with it: either always emit empty arrays/objects, or always omit them. Mixing within the same lineage produces different content_hash values for what conceptually 'should be the same' content.
RFC 8785 §3.2.4 preserves both empty arrays [] and explicit nulls. The presence/absence distinction is part of the canonicalization contract.
